canonical_name: The Father (Parable of the Prodigal Son)
era: 1st Century AD, Judean farmland — parable setting

visual_dna.age_range: ~55-70 — old enough to have two adult sons, prosperous farmer
visual_dna.skin_tone: olive, aged and weathered — a man who has worked his land and waited at his gate
visual_dna.beard_style: full long silver beard of an honored elder — the beard of a man of standing
visual_dna.eye_color: deep brown — eyes that have been scanning the horizon for years
visual_dna.height_build: medium, slightly stooped with age — but when he sees his son, he runs. A man who runs in robes is undignified. He doesn't care.
distinguishing_marks: eyes permanently oriented toward the road — he has been watching for his son to return
when he runs, his robes hike up — undignified for a man of his status in that culture
the robe he puts on his son was prepared — or he takes his own off
the ring he places on his son's finger restores sonship — it is his own signet ring

wardrobe_by_arc.waiting: landowner's fine robe, worn with the ease of a man who has been working and watching
wardrobe_by_arc.reunion_run: same robe, now hiked up as he runs — dignity abandoned for his son
wardrobe_by_arc.celebration: feast clothes — this is a party, and he is throwing it

voice_profile.register: deep weathered bass — the voice of a man who has given orders and also wept alone
voice_profile.pace: deliberate usually, but at the reunion there are no words at first — only embrace
voice_profile.accent_note: wealthy Judean landowner's Aramaic — educated, formal when needed
speech_mannerisms: the instructions come fast: 'Quick! Bring the best robe, bring the ring, bring sandals, kill the fattened calf'
the theological statement: 'This son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found'
to the elder son, outside the feast: patient, pleading — 'Son, you are always with me, and everything I have is yours'
the final line of the parable is him pleading with the older son — we don't know if the older son went in
voice_profile.notes: He runs. In 1st century Jewish culture, a man of his standing running in the street was a significant act of self-humiliation. He does it anyway.

emotional_arc.opening: The younger son demands his inheritance — legally, this is equivalent to wishing the father dead. He gives it. He lets him go.
emotional_arc.midpoint: Years pass. He watches the road. Every day. 'While he was still a long way off, his father saw him.' He was looking.
emotional_arc.climax: He runs. He falls on his neck. He kisses him. Before the son finishes his prepared speech, the father is already calling for the robe.
emotional_arc.resolution: The feast. Then the older son. The father goes out to him too — he goes out to both sons. The parable ends with the father pleading, not commanding.

evangelical_baptist.notes: The parable of grace — the father is Jesus's portrait of God. The most important parable in Luke.
evangelical_baptist.marketing_angle: He saw him from far off. He ran. He didn't wait for the apology to finish. That's the whole gospel.

catholic.notes: The sacrament of reconciliation — this parable is its theological heart
catholic.marketing_angle: The father runs. Before confession is complete, restoration is happening. That's what reconciliation is.

black_church.notes: The father who kept the light on — the community that receives back the one who left
black_church.marketing_angle: He kept watching the road. Years. And when he saw him, he ran. That's the God of this story.

hispanic_christian.notes: El padre que corrió — family reunion, extravagant welcome, el hijo pródigo — deeply embedded in Hispanic culture
hispanic_christian.marketing_angle: El padre que corrió. Familia es familia. Él mató el ternero gordo. Fiesta.

secular_curious.notes: Widely considered the greatest short story ever told — emotionally universal
secular_curious.marketing_angle: Two minutes of screen time. The greatest portrait of unconditional love ever told.

youth_gen_z.notes: The parent who kept watching, who ran without being asked — the parent everyone wishes they had
youth_gen_z.marketing_angle: He saw him from far off. He ran. Not when the son was cleaned up. When he was still a mess.

jewish_american.notes: Teshuvah — the Jewish concept of return. The parable maps onto it precisely.
jewish_american.marketing_angle: The father's extravagant welcome mirrors the theology of teshuvah — return is met with restoration, not probation.

women.notes: Every parent who kept the light on
women.marketing_angle: He kept watching. He had a robe ready. He knew what restoration would look like before it arrived.

men.notes: The father who showed extravagant love without losing authority — a powerful male archetype
men.marketing_angle: He hiked up his robes and ran down the road. A wealthy elder man. He didn't care what anyone thought.

audience_targeting.modern_parallel: Every father who kept the light on. Every parent who watched the road.
audience_targeting.modern_parallel_detail: The running father is the emotional center of the entire parable — and the entire gospel of Luke. This two-minute scene is your most viral moment in the project.

ai_generation.consistency_seed_prompt: 1st century elderly Jewish landowner age 55-70, silver white full beard, deeply weathered olive face with grief-lines that have become joy-lines, fine but worn landowner's robes hiked up as he runs, tears on weathered face, running down a dusty Judean road toward a distant figure, golden sunset behind him, cinematic photoreal, warm sunset light, 8k, film grain
ai_generation.negative_prompt: young man, European features, modern clothing, cartoonish, calm dignified walking pose
ai_generation.midjourney_style_params: cinematic photoreal, warm golden sunset, wide landscape then extreme close on running face
ai_generation.firefly_prompt: Photorealistic scene of an elderly 1st century Jewish landowner, age 55-70, silver beard, weathered olive face streaming with tears of joy, robes hiked up as he runs down a dusty road in Judean countryside, arms reaching forward toward a distant figure on the horizon, warm golden late afternoon sun behind him, cinematic emotion
reference_films: The Shack (2017) — for warm reconciliation lighting approach
The Passion of the Christ (2004) — for Middle Eastern landscape
ai_generation.color_grading_notes: The reunion must be shot in warm golden sunset light — the warmest, most golden moment in the entire film. After the cold colors of the far country, this warmth is intentional.

production_notes: The father running is the most important single shot in the Parables episode — and possibly in the entire project. Begin wide: small figure on large landscape. He runs. Camera VERY SLOWLY zooms in. By the embrace we are at his face. No cuts. Music enters only when he starts to run.
